corelogic_v10561

Let finite sets satisfy $|A|=3,\;|B|=3,\;|C|=3$.
Fix sub-classes $\alpha\subseteq A$ and $\beta\subseteq C$ with
$|\alpha|=|\beta|=2$.

•  $Q\subseteq A\times B$ is a bijection $A\!\to\! B$.  
•  $R\subseteq B\times C$ is a bijection $B\!\to\! C$.

Write the restricted relations  
\[
Q\!\upharpoonright\alpha=\{(a,b)\in Q:a\in\alpha\},\qquad
R\!\upharpoonright\beta=\{(b,c)\in R:c\in\beta\},
\]
and the relative product  
\[
T=(Q\mid R)\!\upharpoonright(\alpha\times\beta)
   =\bigl\{(a,c)\in\alpha\times\beta:\exists b\,(aQb\land bRc)\bigr\}.
\]

**Question.** As $Q$ and $R$ range over all bijections of the stated kinds,
how many *distinct* relations $T$ can arise? Give a single integer.

---

Reasoning (concise)

By *Principia Mathematica* **[PM 43·5]**
\[
(Q\mid R)\!\upharpoonright(\alpha\times\beta)
   \;=\;
(Q\!\upharpoonright\alpha)\mid(R\!\upharpoonright\beta).
\tag{1}
\]

Both $Q\!\upharpoonright\alpha$ and $R\!\upharpoonright\beta$
are bijections between 2-element sets, hence single-valued and onto.
Composing them gives a bijection $\alpha\!\to\!\beta$; there are exactly
two such bijections (the identity pairing and the swap of the two elements).
Every choice of $Q$ and $R$ produces one of these two, and each occurs.

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
